Q1: I tried to set up a consistent study routine, blocking out time in the afternoons when I felt most focused. I'd usually dedicate around 3-4 hours on weekdays to reading through materials and taking detailed notes. But honestly, my anxiety would sometimes derail my plans—I'd sit down to study and then feel overwhelmed by how much there was to cover. I relied heavily on writing things out to process information, so I spent a lot of time creating summaries and rewriting lecture content in my own words.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I tried to keep regular study times, but when deadlines approached, everything went out the window and I'd end up in these intense cramming sessions. Some weeks I was pretty consistent, but other weeks—especially when my anxiety spiked—I'd lose whole days just feeling paralyzed about where to start. The inconsistency frustrated me because I knew I was capable of more.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if assignment feedback had been posted. I'd also log in when I needed to access reading materials or check discussion boards. Sometimes I logged in just to reassure myself I wasn't missing something important—my anxiety made me constantly worried I'd overlooked a deadline or requirement. It became almost a nervous habit to check, even when I knew nothing new had been posted.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of logging in every morning with my coffee to check what was due and review the week's materials. It felt manageable when I had that routine going. But then I missed a few classes due to being unwell, and that threw everything off. I never quite got back to that consistent pattern, which I really regret.
Q5: The written lecture notes and reading materials were my lifeline—I learn best by reading and writing, so having detailed text-based resources was essential. The discussion posts were also helpful because seeing other students' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. I appreciated when instructors posted written feedback on assignments because I could revisit it multiple times and really process what I needed to improve.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch many of the video lectures fully—I'd skim through them or just read the transcripts if they were available. Videos take so long, and I can read the same content much faster and retain it better by making my own notes. Some of the optional practice quizzes I also skipped when I was feeling overwhelmed, even though I knew they'd probably help. I just couldn't add one more thing to my plate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions. When something felt too vague or disconnected from the core material, my anxiety would spike because I felt like I was wasting precious time I didn't have.
Q8: Required activities carried this weight of obligation that sometimes made them feel more stressful, but at least I knew I had to do them. Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford—even when I wanted to engage with them, I'd think about my incomplete required work and feel guilty. It's frustrating because I know the optional stuff could have helped me, but I had to triage constantly.
Q9: Absolutely—I downloaded almost everything and studied from printed notes and PDFs constantly. I'd take the readings to the library or a café where I could focus without the distraction of being online. I also used a couple of textbooks I found at the library and some external YouTube channels that explained concepts differently. A huge portion of my actual studying happened completely offline.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with my printed notes for hours, rewriting summaries and testing myself with flashcards I'd made by hand. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also had study sessions with a couple of classmates where we'd work through problems together using our own notes. The LMS only captured a fraction of the effort I was actually putting in.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download the PDFs and readings in one session, then spend days working through them without logging back in. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without internet distractions. It also meant I could study during my commute or in places without reliable wifi. The LMS would show one quick access, but I'd spend hours with that same material offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof, and I started having trouble sleeping, which made everything worse. I missed some classes because I just couldn't face going in feeling so unprepared and scared. There was also a period where family stuff demanded my attention, and I fell behind in a way that felt impossible to recover from. The CBT sessions helped me develop coping strategies, but it was still a constant battle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or anxious, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—like part-time work and family obligations—meant I sometimes had to choose between attending class and completing assignments. The confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections of the LMS because engaging with them made me feel stupid and more anxious.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the readings open, scrolling through, but my mind would be racing with worry about everything I still needed to do. Sometimes I'd watch discussion posts pile up without really processing what people were saying. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, even when I knew I wasn't really learning in those moments.
Q15: I postponed starting assignments all the time because the fear of not doing them well enough was paralyzing. The bigger or more important something was, the harder it was to begin. I knew this was self-sabotaging, and we worked on it in my CBT sessions, but breaking that pattern is incredibly difficult. Sometimes I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing my incomplete tasks made my anxiety unbearable.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd have periods of low activity, then frantic bursts of logging in repeatedly as deadlines approached. The pressure of deadlines was both motivating and terrifying—it got me moving but also triggered intense anxiety. I wish I could have spread my engagement more evenly, but I kept falling into that last-minute pattern despite my best intentions.
Q17: When I received feedback, it definitely motivated me to engage more—especially positive feedback that showed I was on the right track. But sometimes feedback came too late to really help me adjust for the next assignment. Critical feedback was hard to receive with my anxiety; I'd spiral into self-doubt before I could actually use it constructively. I learned to wait a day before reading feedback so I could process it more calmly.
Q18: The course structure felt a bit overwhelming with everything happening at once. I would have benefited from more scaffolded deadlines rather than having multiple big things due around the same time. The weekly modules were helpful for organizing content, but the flexibility also meant I could fall behind without immediate consequences, which my anxious brain took advantage of in unhealthy ways.
Q19: Having readings and materials clearly organized in the LMS made engagement easier—I always knew where to find things. But the sheer volume of content made it hard to prioritize what was most important. I wished there had been clearer guidance on what to focus on if time was limited. The discussion requirements sometimes felt like busywork when I was already struggling to keep up with core content.
Q20: I think that could easily be someone like me—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, checking things, opening materials, but not actually completing the work because starting feels too overwhelming. High activity might also mean someone is confused and searching for help without finding what they need. It doesn't necessarily mean productive engagement; it could just be digital pacing back and forth.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam score wasn't great, but my assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted work. A lot of my real learning happened offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. Someone could absolutely learn effectively without high LMS activity if they're using downloaded resources or external materials. The LMS is just one window into studying, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—like when someone logs in, how often, what they click on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and what's happening in someone's head. They can't capture offline studying, emotional struggles, or the difference between anxious clicking and genuine learning. They show behavior but not understanding or effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just complete tasks. It's showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, engagement looks like wrestling with difficult concepts until they click, asking questions when confused, and connecting what I'm learning to bigger ideas. It's an internal state that external metrics can only partially capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to keep going despite my anxiety and fear. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes, the study group conversations, or the CBT strategies I used to manage my stress enough to function. It wouldn't capture how much I actually cared about this course even though my final grade doesn't reflect that. My resilience and effort are invisible in those logs, and that's honestly heartbreaking.
